So the first thing I like to share, and I think this is the most common mistake, liking versus unity. So liking says, we say yes to people who are similar to us. Unity says, we say yes to people who belong to us. And just as in the morning I give the same example, which I give now, I say the European soccer cup is coming up. So that's a big thing for us. It's like your... Not the... It's like the Super Bowl. The Super Bowl, thank you, Eili. Or the baseball league. This is a big thing for us because soccer is the number one sport in Europe. And now imagine, what is the request of the behavior? Do you support the Netherlands, the Dutch guys? Of course, because we are all Dutch. Unity. So we all go out there, it's not in the Netherlands this year, we all go out there to a different country, a different place, and we all wear orange and we feel united. We are a unity. Why? We belong to a group of Dutch people and therefore we show the behavior supporting the Dutch team. Now I give an example. In the morning it was received as... Ooh, that's a complicated one. And I especially search for the complicated one because I think we needed to define that subtle nuance here. Imagine now, we are there. A group of orange dressed Dutch people. The behavior is, the required behavior is, do you support the Dutch team? Yes, we are all Dutch, unity. We clearly see that. And then we move to a certain area. Do you show that behavior? Yes, of course, because all the Dutch do that behavior. Unity. The Dutch show in that area we support, it's a unity factor. And I come to social proof in a second. But now I meet an individual. I never met that person. And I didn't request any behavior. It's starting the relationship. What I explained, he starts with an advantage and that has nothing to do with unity. It has to do with liking. He's in a foreign country just like me supporting the Dutch team. He's similar to me. And maybe the big difference is there is no request for behavior. But at that moment it shifts to liking. I like that individual because he's like me. And I believe that's important. You might know, I hired Dr Chaldeany in 2005, and he saw the very big influence challenge at a company I was at. We thought it was liking, but it was unity and I'll explain the situation. So, we were entering a new market. We had no credibility in this market. Okay. And our perceived weakness was we were competing against big carriers and we were the local health plan. So we were the small guy. This insurance is only good in your state. We were getting hammered in the marketplace. And it was our influence challenge, working with Dr Chaldeany when we brought him in. And he told us that's not your weakness, that's your strength. We fell back in our chair. Like, what do you mean we're getting killed in the market on this. And he says, you're, you have a such a strength here, you're, you're, you're their neighbors. You know, they're, you know this better than the big carriers know this. And we actually did a campaign around it. And we wrote music. We wrote an original song. I have it. Okay. We all thought it was liking. We thought it was liking. But it was really unity because we were your neighbors. We live here with you. We're in the same communities as you. We, we live here with you. We have the same insurance plan. We're selling to you. Okay. Now one campaign grew our brand awareness 38%. Wow. We gained three number one market share positions. On the other hand, we say the most difficult thing about influence is knowing exactly what you want to influence. We are not wired to be good at that. That's just, we are not wired to be good at thinking in what behavior do I want to see. So it requires effort. And I know that people who are willing to put in the effort, they are more successful. Because we are also not wired to put in effort in things. A human being is a very interesting species. But I know all of you, you are wired to do that effort. And if you want to do the effort, you can answer a few questions. So the unity is the feeling of togetherness of weakness. And I want to share one research. Maybe Doc Chaldeany discussed this in Arizona. I see if you're not a guest. I know a lot of people don't know it. So what happened? A research is done. There's a group of participants. And three different scenarios. It's a picture on the wall, actually. That's the only thing that's different. One picture is a person standing alone. The other picture is two people standing apart. And the third picture on the wall is people standing together shoulder on shoulder. And then things happen, but by accident, the researcher throws something on the floor. And what they'd like to see is how many of those participants will help pick those items that were accidentally thrown on the floor, pick up. Well, in the standalone image, this is an image, remember, 20% standing apart, approximately the same. But if they saw an image, people standing together shoulder on shoulder, 60% did this. And again, I cannot emphasize this enough. If you go from 20 to 60, this is not a 40% increase of your results. This is 300% increase of the amount of yeses. So this is already interesting. What a single image can do in an environment. You know what we all like. Bob and me love about this research. It's about the participants. You know who the participants were? They were toddlers. Little children. Little children were measured based on an image they saw. How would they react to helping picking up these items? What it tells me at least is how deeply these principles and the feeling of unity is rooted in our DNA, in the core things, how we make decisions. So that's an extra, it's a sign how powerful this is. So unity, blurring boundaries between self and others and forming weaners. So what I like to do for this presentation is helping you, giving it a structure and a process. How can you do it? Well, I think first, and I think the majority of you know this picture, but might not know this question. Ask yourself when you have an influence situation, which picture below describes the relationship with your audience best. I would argue that if you are at the three circles at the bottom, that's some form of unity. So if you're wanting somebody ask a question, what's the difference between you belong to us and you're one of us? Well, maybe it's the circle below in the middle and the circle below at the right. Maybe it's an amplifier. So I said belonging to us could be a very close group and one of us could be family. So I have a research based answer, but I try to explain his way of thinking. What I think is in every situation that you occur, it's a very simple answer. What is the weaners that I have that with that person? So if we form groups, we can be in the same football team, we can be by the same country. We could have this follow the same education. There are multiple ways to form a weaners relation where we often don't think about. So my advice for all of you is answer this question. And if you are able in an ethical way, which is true, move yourself to the circles at the bottom. And I presented or no I prepared and actually Dr. Chaldeany actually prepared this presentation and I honestly stole this from him. Two questions, because like I said in the beginning unity is not a principle that we embrace yet. It takes some time. So we need simple triggers, simple questions. How can we use it? So one way of using it, how can you harness the power of we relationships when it already exists. So you have some form of weaners. Well, the answer is surprisingly easy. You just merely point to them. That's all you have to do. Just like Dr. Chaldeany. Why should I give you that information right now? We belong to the same faculty for over 15 years. Another example was donations on campus. One person went out collecting donations for good cause, serving the campus. And he reached a certain level of money. Then a second person went out collecting donations. But the message was, I'm a student just like you here on campus. We are all students here. And now you see the subtle difference between liking. If it was building liking. Yeah, we all students that has in that increased could increase like ability. It was here the request. Why should I donate because we belong to this group of students on campus and this donation serves us all a 450% increase. That's not bad for one sentence, right? So first step unity, do we have it? Where in those circles are you and then merely point to that. And I love this and I know, I think a lot of you know this example, but why are we. I put it again in this presentation is about Warren Buffett. Every time he addressed his annual shareholder meeting, he used the principles. Look them back. But at one moment and unfortunately Charlie Munger was a good friend of Dr. Chaldeany and and Bobette Gordon. They know Warren Buffett pretty well. Unfortunately Charlie passed away, of course, but before that happened. They got both a lot of questions. You're in your 90s guys, you're great. What you did is amazing. We trust you. But we like some security on our money. There's a lot of money for me invested in Berkshire equity. What's going on? How do you see the future? And he made a frame. I will tell you what I would say to my family today, asked me about Berkshire future. I see two things happening here. One thing is of course the unity frame. You framed all their people as family. So what family does for each other, they take care of each other, they tell the truth. So his message became more credible. That's one thing what I what I really love about studying persuasion is the subtlety of this message. There was no reason to show behavior. He didn't ask for behavior. He created behavior. You see how subtle he was able to raise unity to awareness. It was not the reason why people should do it was raised to awareness and trusting people should do it. That's an A level game of persuasion in my mind. I Lee yeah. Fantastic example. I also like to think about how unity is flexible. And I just want to add that when you feel forced into a decision. That's not going to be a good relationship it's not going to be very long term you're probably going to look for ways to get out of it. You're not going to want to reciprocate in any ways with that person if you feel pushed or forced into that situation. So yeah, you can sometimes you can be effective and get people to do what you want by using force, but it's not going to be a very good long term solution. The much better is to use principles and influence people and inform them into into a decision and I think that gets more cooperation and makes it more long term. With that said, thank you, boss.
